title: Cased stab-bound sketchbook with a cloth cover
subtitle: Combining Japanese stab binding with a traditional cloth case
excerpt: A cased stab-bound sketchbook houses a Japanese-stitch text block inside a standard case-binding cover. The exposed stab stitches on the spine become a decorative element. The structure is rigid and flat-opening, suitable for heavier drawing papers that do not lend themselves to sewn signatures.
sourceNotes: Technique synthesised from Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), public domain, chapter on case-making; and from the Japanese stab-binding tradition documented in early craft-manual sources. The cased stab form combines the Western case structure with the Eastern exposed-spine stitching.

glossaryTerms.definition: The bound pages of a book, considered as a unit separate from its cover. In a cased binding, the text block is made first and then glued into the case.

content.text: Standard Japanese stab bindings are sewn through individual unfolded sheets. This limits them to lighter papers. Heavier cartridge or drawing paper (160-200 gsm) changes this. Housing the
inside a cloth case gives the sketchbook the durability of a case-bound book. The stab-stitch pattern stays visible along the spine.

items.name: Drawing paper, 160-180 gsm, grain short, A5 sheets
items.description: 40 sheets give an 80-page sketchbook. Grain must run parallel to the spine. Hot-press or smooth-surface cartridge takes pencil and pen well; cold-press watercolour paper works for mixed-media use.

items.name: Endpaper stock, 120-140 gsm (slug: endpaper-stock)
items.description: Two sheets folded to A5. The inner half of each endpaper will paste to the board when casing in.

items.name: Bookbinding board, 2 mm (slug: bookbinding-board-2mm)
items.description: Two boards at 155 x 210 mm. Cut to give a 5 mm margin at head, tail, and foredge.

items.name: Bookbinding thread, linen (slug: bookbinding-thread-linen)
items.description: For the stab stitch. 4-cord waxed linen; the stitch is visible so choose a colour that suits the cover.

content.text: Preparing and sewing the text block

Stack all sheets in order with an endpaper at front and back. Jog the stack on a flat surface to align all edges. Mark a piercing template with four sewing stations: 10 mm, 25 mm from head, 25 mm, and 10 mm from tail. Pierce straight through the entire stack from back to front with the bookbinding awl held perpendicular to the sheets. Work slowly; the awl must pass through the same hole in every sheet.

Sew the four-hole yotsume stab stitch: from the back at hole 1 (head), up over the spine edge and down through hole 1 from the front; along the front to hole 2 and down; along the back to hole 3 and up; to hole 4 and down; up over the tail edge; back along the back to hole 3 and up; to hole 2 and down; to hole 1 and up over the head edge. Tie off at hole 1 with a square knot on the back face. This pattern wraps the spine and tail and head edges while passing through each hole twice.

Cut the covering cloth 30 mm larger than the open case on all sides. Glue both boards and the spine piece to the cloth in the correct positions: boards 10 mm in from the cloth edge at head, tail, and foredge; a 10 mm gap between each board and the spine piece. Fold in the cloth turn-ins and mitre the corners. Let dry under a weight for 20 minutes.

Apply PVA to the outer half of each endpaper and press the text block into the open case, centred on the spine piece. The stab stitches should sit against the spine-piece cloth. Close the case, press firmly at the hinges with the bone folder, and weight the closed book for 1 hour. The stab stitches will remain visible along the spine.
